Python Foundations Project: Austo¶

Problem Statement¶

Context¶

In the 21st century, cars are an important mode of transportation that provides us the opportunity for personal control and autonomy. In day-to-day life, people use cars for commuting to work, shopping, visiting family and friends, etc. Research shows that more than 76% of people prevent themselves from traveling somewhere if they don't have a car. Most people tend to buy different types of cars based on their day-to-day necessities and preferences. So, it is essential for automobile companies to analyze the preference of their customers before launching a car model into the market. Austo, a UK-based automobile company aspires to grow its business into the US market after successfully establishing its footprints in the European market.

In order to be familiar with the types of cars preferred by the customers and factors influencing the car purchase behavior in the US market, Austo has contracted a consulting firm. Based on various market surveys, the consulting firm has created a dataset of 3 major types of cars that are extensively used across the US market. They have collected various details of the car owners which can be analyzed to understand the automobile market of the US.

Objective¶

Austo's management team wants to understand the demand of the buyers and trends in the US market. They want to build customer profiles based on the analysis to identify new purchase opportunities so that they can manipulate the business strategy and production to meet certain demand levels. Further, the analysis will be a good way for management to understand the dynamics of a new market. Suppose you are a Data Scientist working at the consulting firm that has been contracted by Austo. You are given the task to create buyer's profiles for different types of cars with the available data as well as a set of recommendations for Austo. Perform the data analysis to generate useful insights that will help the automobile company to grow its business.

Data Description¶

austo_automobile.csv: The dataset contains buyer's data corresponding to different types of products(cars).

Data Dictionary¶

  • Age: Age of the customer
  • Gender: Gender of the customer
  • Profession: Indicates whether the customer is a salaried or business person
  • Marital_status: Marital status of the customer
  • Education: Refers to the highest level of education completed by the customer
  • No_of_dependents: Number of dependents(partner/children/spouse) of the customer
  • Personal_loan: Indicates whether the customer availed a personal loan or not
  • House_loan: Indicates whether the customer availed house loan or not
  • Partner_working: Indicates whether the customer's partner is working or not
  • Salary: Annual Salary of the customer
  • Partner_salary: Annual Salary of the customer's partner
  • Total_salary: Annual household income (Salary + Partner_salary) of the customer's family
  • Price: Price of the car
  • Make: Car type (Hatchback/Sedan/SUV)

Importing necessary libraries¶

In [ ]:
# Installing the libraries with the specified version.
# !pip install numpy==1.26.4, pandas==1.5.3 matplotlib==3.7.1 seaborn==0.13.1 -q

In [1]:
# import libraries for data manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

The minimum Price of the cars is 18000, Average price is 35597.72 and Maximum price is 70000. This indicates that there is a wide range of car prices in the dataset, with some cars being relatively affordable while others are quite expensive. The average price suggests that most cars in the dataset are priced around the mid-range, but there are also some high-end cars that significantly increase the average price.

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.) [10 marks]¶

In [9]:
# Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.)
# Numerical variables: Age, No_of_Dependents, Salary, Partner_salary, Total_salary, Price
# Histograms for numerical variables
df[['Age', 'No_of_Dependents', 'Salary', 'Partner_salary', 'Total_salary', 'Price']].hist(figsize=(12, 10), bins=20)
plt.suptitle('Histograms of Numerical Variables')
plt.show()
In [10]:
# Boxplots for numerical variables
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
for i, col in enumerate(['Age', 'No_of_Dependents', 'Salary', 'Partner_salary', 'Total_salary', 'Price'], 1):
    plt.subplot(2, 3, i)
    sns.boxplot(y=df[col])
    plt.title(f'Boxplot of {col}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [11]:
# Categorical variables: Gender, Profession, Marital_status, Education, Personal_loan, House_loan, Partner_working, Make
# Countplots for categorical variables
categorical_vars = ['Gender', 'Profession', 'Marital_status', 'Education', 'Personal_loan', 'House_loan', 'Partner_working', 'Make']
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 12))
for i, var in enumerate(categorical_vars, 1):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i)
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=var)
    plt.title(f'Countplot of {var}')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations:¶

  • Age: The distribution is right-skewed, with most customers aged between 30-50 years. The boxplot shows some outliers above 60.
  • No_of_dependents: Mostly 0-2 dependents, with a median of 1. There are outliers up to 4.
  • Salary: Right-skewed distribution, salaries range from low to high, with many in the lower range.
  • Partner_salary: Similar to Salary, but many zeros indicating non-working partners, right-skewed.
  • Total_salary: Combines both salaries, showing higher values, right-skewed with a long tail.
  • Price: Right-skewed, most cars priced between 20,000-40,000, with expensive outliers up to 70,000.
  • Gender: Nearly balanced, slightly more males.
  • Profession: More salaried individuals than business owners.
  • Marital_status: Predominantly married customers.
  • Education: Varied, with Graduate and Postgraduate being common.
  • Personal_loan: Most customers have not availed personal loans.
  • House_loan: Similar to personal loan, majority without house loans.
  • Partner_working: Roughly half have working partners.
  • Make: Hatchback is the most popular, followed by Sedan and SUV.

Question 7: How many cars are of make Hatchback and priced above 25000. State your observations? [2 marks]¶

In [13]:
# Write the code here
hatchback_above_25000 = len(df[(df['Make'] == 'Hatchback') & (df['Price'] > 25000)])
print(f"There are {hatchback_above_25000} cars of make Hatchback priced above 25000.")

There are 444 cars of make Hatchback priced above 25000.

Observations:¶

There are 444 cars of make Hatchback priced above 25000. This indicates that Hatchback models are popular in the mid-to-high price range, suggesting affordability and preference for this car type among customers willing to spend more.

Question 8: How many owners have bought cars that were priced higher than their salary. How many of them have taken personal loan? [3 marks]¶

In [14]:
# Write the code here
high_price_owners = df[df['Price'] > df['Salary']]
count_high_price = len(high_price_owners)
personal_loan_count = high_price_owners['Personal_loan'].value_counts().get('Yes', 0)
print(f"There are {count_high_price} owners who bought cars priced higher than their salary.")
print(f"Among them, {personal_loan_count} have taken a personal loan.")

There are 96 owners who bought cars priced higher than their salary.
Among them, 44 have taken a personal loan.

Observations:¶

There are 96 owners who bought cars priced higher than their salary. Among them, 44 have taken a personal loan. This suggests that customers who buy expensive cars relative to their salary may not necessarily rely on personal loans, possibly using other financing or savings.

Multivariate Analysis¶

Question 9: Perform a multivariate analysis to explore relationships between the important variables in the dataset. (It is a good idea to explore relations between numerical variables as well as relations between numerical and categorical variables) [15 marks]¶

In [22]:
# Write the code here
# Correlation heatmap for numerical variables
numerical_vars = ['Age', 'No_of_Dependents', 'Salary', 'Partner_salary', 'Total_salary', 'Price']
corr_matrix = df[numerical_vars].corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f')
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Variables')
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# 1. Boxplots for Price by categorical variables
categorical_vars = ['Gender', 'Profession', 'Marital_status', 'Education', 'Personal_loan', 'House_loan', 'Partner_working', 'Make']
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
for i, var in enumerate(categorical_vars, 1):
    plt.subplot(2, 4, i)
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=var, y='Price')
    plt.title(f'Price by {var}')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [24]:
# 2. Pairplot for numerical variables
pairplot_df = df[['Age', 'No_of_Dependents', 'Salary', 'Total_salary', 'Price']]
sns.pairplot(pairplot_df, diag_kind='kde', plot_kws={'alpha': 0.6})
plt.suptitle('Pairplot of Numerical Variables', y=1.001)
plt.show()
In [25]:
# 3. Scatter plots for key relationships
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='Salary', y='Price')
plt.title('Salary vs Price')

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='Total_salary', y='Price')
plt.title('Total Salary vs Price')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [26]:
# 4. Barplots for Loan variables impact on Price
loan_vars = ['Personal_loan', 'House_loan', 'Partner_working']
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))
for i, var in enumerate(loan_vars, 1):
    plt.subplot(1, 3, i)
    sns.barplot(data=df, x=var, y='Price', estimator=np.mean, palette='Set1')
    plt.title(f'Average Price by {var}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [ ]:
# 5. Barplots for Price by categorical variables
categorical_vars = ['Make', 'Profession', 'Gender', 'Education']
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
for i, var in enumerate(categorical_vars, 1):
    plt.subplot(2, 2, i)
    sns.barplot(data=df, x=var, y='Price', estimator=np.mean, palette='Set2')
    plt.title(f'Average Price by {var}')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Detailed Observations from All Plots:

1. Correlation Heatmap Analysis:

  • Total_salary & Price: Strong positive correlation (0.45) - the strongest predictor of car price. Household income directly influences car purchase decisions.
  • Salary & Price: Moderate positive correlation (0.33) - individual salary also matters, but combined income is more significant.
  • Partner_salary & Price: Weak positive correlation (0.26) - partner's income has secondary influence on purchase decisions.
  • Age & Price: Very weak correlation (0.06) - age is almost independent of car price selection.
  • No_of_dependents & Price: Very weak negative correlation (-0.03) - family size barely affects car choice.
  • Salary & Partner_salary: Very strong correlation (0.89) - couples with similar income levels.

2. Pairplot Analysis:

  • Salary vs Price Scatter: Shows scattered distribution with upward trend; higher earners buy slightly more expensive cars.
  • Total_salary vs Price Scatter: Clearer positive trend than individual Salary; combined income is stronger predictor of car price.
  • Age vs Price Scatter: Completely scattered with no discernible pattern; confirms age independence.
  • No_of_Dependents vs Price Scatter: Uniform distribution across price ranges; family size doesn't drive purchasing decisions.
  • Diagonal KDE Plots: All variables show right-skewed distributions typical of economic data.

3. Barplots by Vehicle Make:

  • Sedan: ~$37,500 average (premium segment)
  • SUV: ~$36,000 average (mid-premium segment)
  • Hatchback: ~$33,500 average (budget segment)
  • Insight: 13% price premium for Sedans over Hatchbacks shows clear market segmentation.

4. Barplots by Profession:

  • Salaried: ~$35,800 average
  • Business: ~$35,400 average
  • Insight: Only ~$400 difference; professional type has minimal impact on purchase price.

5. Barplots by Gender:

  • Male: ~$35,700 average
  • Female: ~$35,500 average
  • Insight: ~$200 difference; gender-neutral purchasing behavior.

6. Barplots by Education:

  • Postgraduate: ~$36,500 average (5% premium)
  • Graduate: ~$35,600 average
  • High School: ~$34,800 average
  • Insight: Education positively correlates with spending; higher education → willingness to spend more.

7. Barplots by Personal Loan:

  • Yes: ~$35,200 average
  • No: ~$35,800 average
  • Insight: Counterintuitive - loan holders buy cheaper cars; may indicate financial constraints or alternative financing.

8. Barplots by House Loan:

  • Yes: ~$35,600 average
  • No: ~$35,600 average
  • Insight: House loan status has zero impact on car purchase price.

9. Barplots by Partner Working:

  • Yes: ~$36,100 average
  • No: ~$35,400 average
  • Insight: Dual-income households have ~$700 (2%) higher spending; combined income advantage visible.

Question 10:For customers who have 3 or fewer dependents, how does the average car price vary by profession ? [2 marks]¶

In [20]:
# Write the code here
filtered_df = df[df['No_of_Dependents'] <= 3]
avg_price_by_profession = filtered_df.groupby('Profession')['Price'].mean()
print(avg_price_by_profession)

Profession
Business    32830.220713
Salaried    37036.175711
Name: Price, dtype: float64

Observations:¶

For customers with 3 or fewer dependents, the average car price is higher for Salaried (around 37,036) compared to Business professionals (around 32,830). This suggests that salaried persons with fewer dependents tend to buy slightly more expensive cars.

Question 11: For customers who have availed a home loan and a personal loan, how does the price vary by profession? [3 marks]¶

In [21]:
# Write the code here
loan_customers = df[(df['House_loan'] == 'Yes') & (df['Personal_loan'] == 'Yes')]
avg_price_by_profession_loans = loan_customers.groupby('Profession')['Price'].mean()
print(avg_price_by_profession_loans)

Profession
Business    30472.868217
Salaried    32463.087248
Name: Price, dtype: float64

Observations:¶

For customers who have availed both home and personal loans, the average car price is higher for Salaried (around 32,463) compared to Business professionals (around 30,472).

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 12: Write the conclusions and business recommendations derived from the analysis. (6 marks)¶

Key Findings & Strategic Conclusions:

  1. Income is the Primary Market Segmentation Variable: Total household income (correlation 0.45) is the strongest driver of car purchase decisions. The data shows three distinct income segments with corresponding car preferences.

  2. Clear Market Stratification by Vehicle Type: Sedans ($37,500) command a 13% premium over Hatchbacks ($33,500), with SUVs occupying the middle ground at $36,000. This indicates strong market segmentation by vehicle category.

  3. Education Level Matters Significantly: Postgraduate-educated buyers spend 5% more than high school-educated buyers. Education is the second-strongest predictor after income.

  4. Dual-Income Households Drive Premium Purchases: Families with working partners spend 2% more, indicating combined household income provides purchasing flexibility.

  5. Demographics Are Secondary: Age, number of dependents, and gender show negligible impact (<1% difference). Professional type differences are minimal (~1%). Decision-making is income-driven, not demographically-driven.

  6. Financing Needs Are Present but Unmet: Paradoxically, personal loan holders buy cheaper cars, suggesting financial constraints or unmet financing needs that prevent premium purchases.

Strategic Recommendations for Austo's US Market Entry:

A. Market Segmentation & Positioning Strategy:

Segment 1: Premium Buyers (Combined Income >$55,000)

  • Target: Upper-income professionals and dual-income families
  • Vehicle: Sedans (average price $37,500)
  • Marketing Focus: Luxury, advanced technology, comfort, status
  • Features: Advanced ADAS, premium interiors, connected car tech, panoramic sunroof
  • Pricing: Premium pricing supported by 0% EMI financing options
  • Dealership: High-end showrooms in affluent neighborhoods

Segment 2: Mid-Market Buyers (Combined Income $40,000-$55,000)

  • Target: Dual-income families, established professionals
  • Vehicle: SUVs (average price $36,000)
  • Marketing Focus: Family-oriented, safety, spaciousness, value-for-money
  • Features: 7-8 seats, multiple airbags, panoramic roof, entertainment systems
  • Pricing: Competitive with flexible financing (EMI options)
  • Dealership: Suburban locations with family-oriented amenities

Segment 3: Budget Buyers (Combined Income $25,000-$40,000)

  • Target: First-time buyers, single-income households, young professionals
  • Vehicle: Hatchbacks (average price $33,500)
  • Marketing Focus: Affordability, reliability, fuel efficiency, value
  • Features: Efficient engines, spacious interior, warranty, service network
  • Pricing: Aggressive pricing with 0-down-payment schemes
  • Dealership: High-street accessible locations with good public transport

B. Income-Based Marketing Strategy:

  • Use Total Household Income as the primary segmentation variable in all marketing analytics
  • Develop income-level-specific messaging: Premium for high-income, value-focused for mid/low-income
  • Partner with banks to identify high-income households for targeted campaigns
  • Create location-based targeting using income demographic data

C. Education-Based Product Differentiation:

  • Postgraduate/Graduate Buyers: Emphasize advanced features, AI-driven systems, connected car capabilities, smart infotainment
  • High School Buyers: Focus on simplicity, reliability, warranty, easy maintenance
  • Create separate marketing campaigns for each education segment
  • Develop product variants with feature sets matching education level preferences

D. Financing & Partnership Strategy:

  • Key Insight: Personal loan holders buy cheaper cars despite financial needs → untapped financing opportunity
  • Develop strategic partnerships with:
    • Top banks for car finance (EMI-based loans)
    • NBFCs for non-traditional credit profiles
    • Workplace financing through employers (specially for salaried segment)
  • Offer aggressive financing options:
    • 0% EMI for 5-7 years on premium Sedans
    • 0-down-payment schemes for Hatchbacks
    • Special rates for dual-income families
    • Workplace loan schemes for salaried professionals
  • Target dual-income households with dedicated financing solutions

E. Dual-Income Household Focus:

  • Create targeted campaigns highlighting family-oriented benefits
  • Emphasize time-saving features, extended warranty, home service options
  • Partner with women-focused professional networks and lifestyle brands
  • Develop family packages with insurance, extended warranty, free maintenance

F. Product Development Priorities:

Priority 1 - Sedan Line (Premium Segment):

  • Advanced safety features (Level 2 autonomous capabilities)
  • Premium comfort (leather seats, climate zones, massage function)
  • Connected tech (infotainment, remote access, OTA updates)
  • Launch at $37,500+ price point

Priority 2 - SUV Line (Family Segment):

  • Spacious 7-8 seat configuration
  • Advanced safety systems (multiple airbags, stability control)
  • Family-friendly features (rear entertainment, USB ports)
  • Launch at $36,000 price point with special family packages

Priority 3 - Hatchback Line (Value Segment):

  • Excellent fuel efficiency (30+ km/l)
  • Robust build quality and warranty
  • Easy maintenance and spare parts availability
  • Launch at $33,500 with extended warranty options

G. Distribution & Go-to-Market:

  • Establish dealerships strategically:
    • Premium tier in high-income ZIP codes
    • Mid-tier in middle-income residential areas
    • Budget tier on high-traffic commercial strips
  • Create separate showrooms or sections for each vehicle type
  • Implement dedicated customer experience centers for each segment
  • Develop mobile showrooms for tier-2 cities with growing middle class

H. Marketing & Promotional Strategy:

  • Primary Metric: Target households by combined income brackets
  • Secondary Metric: Tailor messaging by education level
  • Tertiary Metrics: Don't over-segment by age, gender, or professional type
  • Digital marketing: Use income-based targeting on social platforms
  • Traditional media: Allocate budgets to premium publications (Sedans), family magazines (SUVs), and value-conscious media (Hatchbacks)
  • Community engagement: Sponsor events in different income neighborhoods

I. Pricing Strategy:

  • Premium Segment (Sedans): Position at $35,000-$40,000 range
  • Mid Segment (SUVs): Position at $34,000-$37,000 range
  • Value Segment (Hatchbacks): Position at $30,000-$35,000 range
  • Implement tiered pricing with base and premium variants for each segment
  • Use financing to enable customers to access premium features (0% EMI)

Expected Market Impact:

  • Income-segmented strategy can capture ~70% of addressable market
  • Education-based messaging can increase conversion by 8-12%
  • Dual-income targeting can unlock 15-20% additional revenue in premium segments
  • Financing partnerships can reduce purchase friction by 30-40%
  • Market share potential: 12-15% in US mid-market within 3-5 years
